name: GLUT1 affinity for glucose
tier_rationale: Tier B from Illsley 2000 Placenta review (PMID 10692246): GLUT1 is the primary syncytial transporter for trans-placental glucose; present on both microvillous and basal membranes with the basal acting as rate-limiting step. Km in the 2-5 mmol/L range is characteristic of the facilitated-diffusion family at physiological glucose.

applicability.population: Human term syncytiotrophoblast microvillous membrane vesicles

notes: Michaelis constant for GLUT1-mediated glucose transport on the syncytiotrophoblast microvillous membrane.

name: GLUT1 maximum transport rate per unit area
tier_rationale: Tier C from Illsley 2000 Placenta review: GLUT1 Vmax estimated from vesicle-protein-corrected microvillous-membrane data. Translation to per-area-of-villous-surface introduces extrapolation error.
notes: Maximum facilitated-diffusion flux per unit villous surface area via GLUT1. Caveats: Vmax extrapolated from vesicle-protein assays scaled to whole-placenta villous surface area; tier C reflects the extrapolation.

name: GLUT3 affinity for glucose
tier_rationale: Tier B from Baumann 2002 Endocrine review (PMID 12583599) summarising placental glucose-transporter kinetics. GLUT3 is the high-affinity isoform (Km ~1-2 mmol/L) and is localised to the syncytial microvillous membrane (confirmed by Brown 2011 PMID 22000473). The asymmetric GLUT distribution (microvillous >> basal) makes the basal membrane the rate-limiting step.

source_check.source_citation: Schmidl et al. 2020, Front. Mol. Biosci.
source_check.quote: GLUT3 exhibits a high affinity for glucose (KM = 1.4 mM)
source_check.extracted_value: Review of the human GLUT transporters states GLUT3 is the high-affinity isoform with Km = 1.4 mM for glucose.

notes: Michaelis constant for GLUT3-mediated glucose transport on the syncytiotrophoblast basal membrane.

name: GLUT3 maximum transport rate per unit area
tier_rationale: Tier C: Vmax extrapolated from microvillous-membrane GLUT3 protein-expression data (Brown 2011 PMC3272879). Brown 2011 confirms GLUT3 is on the syncytiotrophoblast microvillous membrane and that expression declines through gestation (2nd trimester = 48±7% of 1st, 3rd trimester = 34±10% of 1st). Absolute Vmax inference from relative expression is the Tier-C step; the underlying expression data is robustly measured (n=8 per trimester, Western blot, siRNA-validated).
notes: Maximum facilitated-diffusion flux per unit villous surface area via GLUT3. Caveats: GLUT3 expression rises through gestation; this is a term-equivalent reference value.

name: Maternal-fetal glucose concentration gradient at term
tier_rationale: Tier B from Illsley 2000: at term, fetal plasma glucose runs approximately 70-75% of maternal, giving a maternal-fetal gradient of ~1.0-1.5 mmol/L under fasting conditions. The gradient arises from rapid fetal glucose utilisation against a finite GLUT1/GLUT3 trans-placental transport ceiling. The gradient widens transiently after maternal feeding and narrows during prolonged fasting.

source_check.source_citation: Holme et al. 2015, PLOS ONE (human in vivo)
source_check.quote: The transplacental maternal-fetal glucose gradient was 1.22 (0.42) mmol/L.
source_check.extracted_value: Human in vivo study of healthy term pregnancies measured the transplacental maternal-fetal glucose gradient as 1.22 (SD 0.42) mmol/L.

applicability.population: Healthy singleton pregnancies, term, mother fasted

notes: Couples to fetal_metabolism.fetal_glucose_term_mmol_per_l and the maternal glucose dataset entry; consistent with the canonical fetal/maternal ratio ~0.7.

name: Net trans-placental glucose flux per kg fetal weight at term
tier_rationale: Tier B from Illsley 2000: net trans-placental glucose flux at term is approximately 4-7 mg/kg/min, matching the fetal glucose utilisation rate already in the dataset and one of the more robustly characterised fetal-substrate fluxes. The flux is largely facilitated diffusion down the maternal-fetal concentration gradient via GLUT1 (basal microvillous) and GLUT3 (apical microvillous) transporters.

source_check.source_citation: Rao et al. 2013, Indian J. Endocrinol. Metab.
source_check.quote: Fetal glucose utilization rates (5-7 mg/kg/min) are higher than in adults (2-3 mg/kg/min).
source_check.extracted_value: Clinical review of in utero fuel homeostasis states fetal glucose utilization rates are 5-7 mg/kg/min, higher than adult rates of 2-3 mg/kg/min.

notes: Matches fetal_metabolism.glucose_utilisation_mg_per_kg_per_min as expected from the steady-state assumption (the fetus stores essentially no glucose).
